content.text: Regulatory and Customer Notification for Major Incidents

When a major incident occurs on an AI platform, the obligation to notify does not stop at the status page. Depending on what data was affected, which customers were impacted, and whether the incident involved a safety-relevant failure, there may be legal notification requirements with hard deadlines, contractual SLA breach notifications, and enterprise customer outreach protocols. Managing these correctly protects both the company and its users.

This sub-subtopic provides a framework for thinking about notification obligations. It is not legal advice. Always engage Legal and Compliance before making regulatory notifications. Specific obligations vary significantly by jurisdiction, data type, and contract terms.
The Notification Landscape
Major incident notifications fall into three categories with different drivers, timelines, and audiences:
Category
Driver
Audience
Typical Timeline
Regulatory notification
Law (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA, SOC 2 breach provisions)
Government/regulatory body
24-72 hours from discovery
Contractual notification
Enterprise SLA or DPA clause
Enterprise customers
Per contract, usually 24-48h
Proactive customer notification
Trust and transparency best practice
Affected users / all customers
As soon as impact is confirmed
These are independent obligations. A major incident may trigger all three, some, or none. The assessment of which apply must happen within the first hour of a declared SEV-1.

attrs.alt: Regulatory and Customer Notification Decision Tree and Timeline

content.text: Regulatory Notification Obligations
GDPR (EU/UK)

Under GDPR, a personal data breach that is 'likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons' must be notified to the relevant supervisory authority (the Data Protection Authority, or DPA) within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach. If the breach is likely to result in a high risk, affected individuals must also be notified without undue delay.
For AI platforms, the threshold is triggered when:
The incident exposed personal data to unauthorised parties (e.g., a caching bug caused one user's AI session data to be visible to another)
The incident caused loss of availability of personal data where availability is required (e.g., medical AI system unavailability affecting patient care)
The incident resulted in AI-generated outputs that could harm individuals if they were based on or expose personal data

The 72-hour clock starts from when the organisation became aware of the breach — which is when it was discovered, not when it was investigated fully. Partial information is sufficient to notify; the notification can be supplemented later.
HIPAA (US Healthcare)
For AI systems handling protected health information (PHI), HIPAA breach notification rules require:
Notification to affected individuals: within 60 days of discovery
Notification to the Department of Health and Human Services (HHS): within 60 days if fewer than 500 individuals affected; annual summary report if more
Notification to prominent media: if more than 500 residents of a state are affected

HIPAA applies a different definition: a breach is presumed to have occurred unless the organisation can demonstrate a low probability that the PHI was compromised, based on a four-factor risk assessment.
SOC 2 and ISO 27001

These are certification frameworks, not laws, but many enterprise contracts require SOC 2 compliance and include specific incident notification requirements. Review the customer's contract to determine whether the incident triggers a contractual SOC 2 breach notification obligation separate from the SLA notification.
EU AI Act (Emerging)

The EU AI Act (fully applicable from August 2026) introduces notification requirements for serious incidents involving high-risk AI systems. 'Serious incident' includes incidents that result in death, serious injury, significant property damage, or serious impact on fundamental rights. For AI platforms operating in the EU, understand which of your AI systems are classified as high-risk under the Act before an incident occurs — not during one.
Enterprise Customer Contractual Notifications
Reading the Contract Before the Incident

The worst time to discover a contractual notification obligation is during a SEV-1 at 2am. Before any major incident occurs, the engineering team should have a pre-populated table of notification requirements for each Tier-1 enterprise customer:

# Maintained by Legal + Customer Success; reviewed quarterly
tier1_customer_notification_requirements:
  - customer: "Acme Corp"
    sla_uptime: 99.9%
    notification_trigger: "Any downtime > 15 min affecting Acme's tenant"
    notification_window: "2 hours from incident start"
    notification_contact: "sre-incidents@acme.com + account_mgr@acme.com"
    format_required: "Email with incident ID and initial impact assessment"
    post_incident_report: "Required within 5 business days"
    contract_ref: "MSA Section 8.3, Exhibit B"

  - customer: "Global Health Systems"
    sla_uptime: 99.95%
    notification_trigger: "Any incident affecting PHI access or availability"
    notification_window: "1 hour from incident start"
    notification_contact: "security@globalhealth.com + legal@globalhealth.com"
    format_required: "Structured incident report per Exhibit C template"
    post_incident_report: "Required within 48 hours with preliminary RCA"
    contract_ref: "BAA Section 4, MSA Section 9.1"
This table is stored in the on-call runbook, not in a document that requires navigating a Confluence hierarchy during an incident.
What the Contractual Notification Must Include
For most enterprise contracts, the initial notification requires:
Incident identifier and declaration time
Services affected (in terms the customer recognises, not internal service names)
Nature of the impact (availability, data, performance)
Current status and estimated time to resolution
Next update time
What it must NOT include (in the initial notification):
Root cause speculation
— say 'we are investigating' rather than guessing; incorrect early attributions create liability
Remediation commitments
— do not commit to a fix timeline that engineering has not confirmed
Internal details
— no mention of internal service names, architecture details, or team names
Proactive Customer Communication
Status Page as Baseline
The public status page update (see the Status Page Comms subtopic) is the baseline for all customers. Enterprise-specific notifications are additive, not replacements for the status page.
Tiered Outreach Protocol
Not every incident warrants direct customer outreach. Apply a tiered protocol:
Tier
Criteria
Outreach method
Timeline
Tier 1: Direct phone
Enterprise customer with SLA breach + direct business impact confirmed
Account manager phones customer contact
Within 2h of incident declaration
Tier 2: Direct email
Enterprise customer affected but SLA not yet breached; or broad customer-facing incident
Templated email from account manager
Within 4h of incident declaration
Tier 3: Status page only
Self-serve customers; incident resolved < 30 min; no data affected
Status page update only
As soon as impact confirmed
Post-Incident Customer Report
For Tier-1 and Tier-2 incidents, send a post-incident report (PIR) to affected enterprise customers. The PIR is not the full engineering postmortem — it is a customer-facing summary:

Subject: Post-Incident Report: [Service] Disruption -- [Date] [INC-ID]

Dear [Customer Contact],

On [date], [service name] experienced a disruption from [start time] to
[end time] ([N] hours [M] minutes). This report summarises what happened,
how it was resolved, and what we are doing to prevent recurrence.

**Impact**
- Service affected: [plain language description]
- Your account impact: [specific to their usage if known]
- Duration: [N] hours [M] minutes
- SLA credit applicable: Yes / No ([credit calculation per contract])

**What Happened**
[2-3 plain-language sentences. No jargon.]

**How We Resolved It**
[1-2 sentences on mitigation steps taken]

**What We Are Doing to Prevent Recurrence**
1. [Action item 1 -- owner, due date]
2. [Action item 2 -- owner, due date]

We value your trust and are committed to the reliability standards in our
agreement. If you have questions, please contact [account manager] at
[email/phone].

[Signature]
AI-Specific Notification Considerations
Model Output Incidents
Incidents where the AI produced harmful, incorrect, or sensitive outputs raise notification questions that traditional infrastructure incidents do not:
If the AI produced outputs that exposed or inferred personal data about one user to another, that may be a GDPR-notifiable breach even if no infrastructure was compromised
If the AI produced harmful content at scale (content policy violation), affected users may need to be notified — but the notification must be carefully worded to avoid amplifying the harm

If the AI's outputs were used in a regulated context (medical diagnosis, financial advice), the notification obligation may include the regulated entity (hospital, financial institution) in addition to the end user
For all model output incidents, engage Legal before any notification to determine whether the incident constitutes a notifiable breach under applicable law.
Algorithmic Decisions Affecting Individuals

In some jurisdictions, if an AI system makes automated decisions that significantly affect individuals (loan approvals, content moderation, employment screening), there may be obligations to notify affected individuals of the automated decision and their right to human review. An incident that causes incorrect automated decisions at scale may create both an incident notification obligation and a retroactive individual decision notification obligation.
Notification Anti-Patterns and Failures
Anti-pattern
What goes wrong
Correct approach
Silent period
No status page update for 2+ hours while team investigates internally
Post 'Investigating' within 30 minutes even with no root cause known
Over-disclosure in initial notification
Early email speculates root cause incorrectly; creates liability and distrust when corrected
State only confirmed facts; use 'we are investigating' for unknown elements
Notification without Legal for regulatory incidents
Notification to DPA triggers follow-up audit; team is unprepared
Legal leads all regulatory notifications; engineering provides technical facts
Treating all customers the same
Enterprise customer with SLA learns about breach from public status page
Tier-1 customers receive direct outreach before or simultaneous with status page
No follow-up PIR
Customer memory of incident is shaped by their experience during it, not resolution
PIR within 48h closes the trust gap; shows the team took it seriously
Premature all-clear
System declared restored before full verification; brief second incident follows
Confirm all systems stable for 30+ min before posting 'Resolved' to status page
Building the Notification Capability Before Incidents Occur
Notification capability cannot be built during a SEV-1. The three assets that must exist before any major incident:
1. Customer Impact Registry

A queryable registry that maps service components to the customer segments they affect. During an incident, the IC should be able to answer 'which enterprise customers are affected by this component going down?' in under 2 minutes:
2. Pre-Approved Notification Templates
Drafting customer-facing language during an incident under time pressure produces poor communication. Pre-approve templates for the most common scenarios:
API availability incident
(service down or severely degraded)
Data latency incident
(service available but responses delayed)
Partial outage
(subset of features or regions affected)
Data incident
(potential data exposure or loss — must include 'contact Legal before sending' header)
Safety incident
(model output policy violation — must include 'contact Legal + Safety before sending' header)

Templates are reviewed by Legal and Customer Success quarterly and updated whenever messaging guidelines change. During an incident, the comms liaison fills in the blanks rather than drafting from scratch.
3. Regulatory Obligation Map
A one-page reference that maps the team's services to their regulatory scope, maintained by Legal and reviewed at each new enterprise customer onboarding:

Regulatory Obligation Quick Reference (Engineering Copy)
Last reviewed: [date] | Owner: Legal@

Service               | GDPR scope | HIPAA scope | SOC2 | AI Act (high-risk)
----------------------|------------|-------------|------|-------------------
inference-api         | YES (EU)   | YES (PHI)   | YES  | REVIEW PENDING
embedding-service     | YES (EU)   | NO          | YES  | NO
content-filter        | NO         | NO          | YES  | YES (safety system)
user-data-api         | YES (ALL)  | REVIEW      | YES  | NO

If an incident affects a YES service: notify Legal on-call immediately.
Legal on-call pager: [number] | backup: [number]
DPA contacts by country: [link to internal doc]
HIPAA breach assessment form: [link]
The Notification Runbook
Create a notification runbook that the on-call team and comms liaison can execute during an incident without waiting for Legal to be available:

Notification Runbook (SEV-1, execute within first hour)

Step 1: Assess scope (5 min)
  [ ] Personal data affected? -> If YES: notify Legal immediately
  [ ] Enterprise customers affected? -> If YES: check tier1_notifications.yaml
  [ ] AI safety event? -> If YES: notify AI Safety team lead immediately

Step 2: Status page (T+30m target)
  [ ] Post "Investigating" status for affected services
  [ ] Link: [status page admin URL]
  [ ] Notify: comms-liaison@ for wording review

Step 3: Enterprise outreach (T+2h target)
  [ ] For each affected Tier-1 customer in tier1_notifications.yaml:
      [ ] Account manager notified via Slack #incident-[ID]
      [ ] Initial email sent from account_manager@ with template IM-01
      [ ] Confirmation logged in incident tracker

Step 4: Regulatory triage (T+1h target)
  [ ] Legal on-call notified if GDPR/HIPAA/CCPA scope
  [ ] Legal decision recorded: notify / monitor / no action required
  [ ] If notify: Legal leads; engineering provides technical facts only

Step 5: Document all notifications
  [ ] Add notification log to incident tracker:
      [timestamp] [type] [recipient] [method] [content-summary]
Summary

Major incident notifications are not a single action but a set of parallel obligations with different timelines, audiences, and content requirements. Regulatory notifications (GDPR: 72h, HIPAA: 60 days) are driven by law; contractual notifications (typically 24-48h) are driven by enterprise SLA agreements; proactive customer communications are driven by trust. For AI platforms, model output incidents and algorithmic decision failures may create notification obligations that traditional infrastructure incidents do not. The most important preparation is pre-incident: a pre-populated table of enterprise customer notification requirements, a notification runbook executable during an incident, and Legal engaged before any regulatory notification is made.
Key takeaways:
Assess notification obligations within 1 hour of SEV-1 declaration -- timelines run from discovery, not resolution
GDPR: 72h to DPA if personal data breach with likely risk. HIPAA: 60 days for PHI breach
Keep a pre-populated table of enterprise customer notification requirements in the on-call runbook
Initial notifications state impact and status -- never speculate on root cause or commit to fix timelines
AI model output incidents may create notification obligations even when no infrastructure failed
